title: Rizqi Ramadhan
text: Hi! My name is Rama, short for Rizqi Ramadhan, and I am a PhD student under the supervision of Chikaraishi Sensei at the Urban and Transportation Planning Lab (UTPL) of Hiroshima University. My research focuses on the travel behavior of households having dual caregiving responsibilities. I am passionate about understanding how to capture trips that derived from other person’s needs and howcaregiving roles influence travel patterns. I am excited to share my research progress and projects on this website, and I welcome any feedback or collaboration opportunities.

title: Evaluating stakeholder structure and private actor involvement on the distribution of relief goods considering a partly centralised decision
Evaluating stakeholder structure and private actor involvement on the distribution of relief goods considering a partly centralised decision
Evaluating stakeholder structure and private actor involvement on the distribution of relief goods considering a partly centralised decision
text:
Abstract
Relief distribution involves numerous stakeholders with various roles and different structures regarding stakeholders’ coordination. This paper proposes a model to evaluate various stakeholder structures for distributing relief goods. Unlike previous research, this model evaluates the coordination structure, assuming no single centralised decision-maker exists. The proposed model is developed based on the decomposition-based framework, assuming a partly centralised decision. The proposed model also integrates road network restoration into the model by capturing the disrupted road network. The role of the private sector is elaborated by exploring the impact of private business involvement in food-related distribution and road network restoration. The proposed model is applied to an actual case, namely the Palu earthquake and tsunami in 2018. We found that the multifocal structure performs better in distributing relief goods. Furthermore, the involvement of the private sector succeeds in minimising the unsatisfied demand.

text:
Progress ReportResearch DocumentationFeedback and Action Items

Reporting my reading progress

2. Current Situation

What I did

Reading literatures on the institutionalization of care services
Trying to translate theories and findings from the literature into survey questions



What I could not do / What is delayed

Fixating the questions required for improving
Developing mathematical models for the alternative total economic output calculation




3. Where I Am Stuck / What I Can Not Decide on My Own

The questions I can fixate on the improved travel survey
How to translate findings from previous research into survey questions
Reference of methods to calculate the economic output of the care services



4. Things I Want to Confirm

The initial conceptual framework





Pick-Up and Drop-Off Trips as Caregiving Activity
from measuring social welfare to the potential of formalizing care services


Increasing trend of dependent regardless demographic switch
The betterment of life quality supporting system prolongs the life expectancy of the population nowadays. In parallel, the fertility rate shows a declining trend. These demographic changes have reduced the aggregate burden of child rearing, while increasing the burden of elder care.
For example, I attached trends of dependent population from Japan, US, and Sweden. Across all three countries, the under-15 share has declined steadily while the 65+ share has risen. This pattern expected to continue through 2040.

Note



“Dependency ratio, an important indicator of the amount of time, energy, and money the working age population must devote to non workers.”
— Folbre and Nelson (2000)





Measuring household time allocation

beneficial to understand how tasks and resources are allocated within a household.
The most common method is by utilizing time use surveys which usually collected by national bureaus or agencies
Miller and Sedai (2021) broaden the data source by using panel-time diaries to account the effect of time-varying variables



(Older) Household heads had to reduce time allocated for other than caregiving

For every additional hour of caregiving activities, study from Miller and Sedai (2021) shows that allocated time for work, leisure, home production, and personal care are reduced.
The panel time-use data shows that women have a greater time reduction on caring for self rather than men, thus inflicting health degradation.
We are also interested if the reduction of other life aspects because of non-spousal care also applies to younger household heads.



Treating PUDO trips as unpaid caregiving activities can be a first step to understand relationship between providing care and social welfare

Using Becker’s (1965) time allocation framework, we can see that the increasing care needs (increasing duration of caregiving) of a household member will effectively raises providers’ marginal benefit of giving care.
Caregiving activity includes all unpaid physical, medical, and school related assistance to others which often is a time intensive task (Miller and Sedai, 2021)



Potential of increased total economic production by putting nonmarket care-related activities into calculation

Folbre and Nelson (2000) argues the economy will seem to grow if we put value on non-market family-related or care-related activities and include them in calculation
Robert Eisner (1989) adds that the working hours of nonmarket activities accounts a significant amount of 40% to 60% from the total US output value
The Europe Union made effort to develop alternative income calculation by utilizing time-use data of activities
Calculation can also pushed further by taking account the value of household capital and raw materials (Ironmonger, 1996)



The Idea of Gross Economic Product by Ironmonger (1996)
\[GEP = GHP + GMP\]
Where:
\[GHP = W_H + K_H = \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} h_i \cdot w\right) + \left(K_{eq} + K_{hous}\right)\]
\[GMP = GDP - K_{hous}\]
\[GEP = \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} h_i \cdot w\right) + K_{eq} + K_{hous} + GDP - K_{hous} = \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} h_i \cdot w\right) + K_{eq} + GDP\]
Variable descriptions:







Symbol
Description




\(GEP\)
Gross Economic Product — total value added by both household and market sectors


\(GHP\)
Gross Household Product — value added by unpaid household activities


\(GMP\)
Gross Market Product — GDP adjusted to remove double-counted owner-occupied housing


\(GDP\)
Conventional Gross Domestic Product as measured by the SNA


\(W_H\)
Value of unpaid household labor (~83% of GHP in Australia 1992)


\(h_i\)
Hours worked per week in household industry \(i\) (e.g. meal prep, child care, laundry)


\(w\)
Imputed wage rate per hour (AUD 14.25/hr in 1992)


\(n\)
Number of household industries (8 in the paper’s classification)


\(K_H\)
Total household capital contribution to GHP


\(K_{eq}\)
Imputed rental value of household equipment and vehicles


\(K_{hous}\)
Imputed rental value of owner-occupied housing (deducted from GDP to avoid double-counting)





The shift from household task to monetized service

Increasing trend of women participation in paid employment is believed by Folbre and Nelson (2000) as one aspect triggering the commodification of traditionally domestic tasks, including caring and homemaking activities
This is usually the case when doing more homemaking activities tends to lower parents earnings, thus indicating the increasing demand of care services due to longer paid working hours.
There is also another case when reallocate their non-market time and effort away from material production towards the personal and emotional actions



Factors affecting demand of paid care service (Folbre and Nelson, 2000)

Encouraging factors

expected freedom from doing bulk number of tedious homemaking activities
provide better quality of service, if:

caring activities seemed as matter of choice rather than necessity
costs being more widely and equitably distributed
get care services from those specially trained

enhance recipients’ sense of life control



Discouraging factors

Unable to replace personal care services that highly value the identity of provider and the continuity of the relationship
It is not guaranteed whether market competition will produce high quality care.
The market of care services may not be affordable equally across diverse groups.
Acute capitalization will make care services impersonal, thus produced at minimum cost and sold to the highest bidder




Factors affecting supply of paid care service (Morgan, 2005)

Encouraging factors

Responsibility of care is being left to private markets
Enormous supply of low-wage and low-skilled workers
Lack of unionization that could push wages higher and promote better working condition



Discouraging factors

Markets, even for services, are strictly controlled by government
Workforce rights are highly protected:

generous parental leave benefit
relatively high minimum wage
requirement of receiving specific vocational education

strong influence from worker unions




(Proposed) Research Question
This research proposal is currently structured around two interconnected phase/inquiries:

RQ 1

How to utilize PUDO trips as an alternative measurement of economic production



RQ 2

What factors causing PUDO trips as a care service hard to institutionalized?




(Proposed) Conceptual Framework





flowchart TD
    A["Driving others as caregiving activity"]
    A --> B["Capturing trips done for other's purpose"]

    B --> B1["Measure time and resource allocation"]
    B --> B2["Capturing emotional and interrelation characteristics"]

    B1 --> B1a
    B2 --> B2a

    subgraph C["Improved travel-diary surveys"]
        B1a["1st layer questions"]
        B2a["2nd layer questions"]
    end

    B1a --> B1b["Developing alternative calculation\nof total economic production"]
    B2a --> B2b["Measuring the potential of marketization"]








(Proposed) Additional questions on Travel Diary Surveys

Layer 1: Capturing the trip

For whom is this trip done?
Is this trip your main activity? Or were you planning to go somewhere else before picking up / after dropping off?



Layer 2: Acknowledging the emotional relationship

Do you drive others because it is your responsibility?
Do you feel this activity will become a burden to others if you avoid or cancel?
Is this driving activity actively involved in building emotional relationships?
Is this activity reducing or included at the intended time?
For the passenger: “Do you feel losing control of your life when driven somewhere?”




References

Becker, G. S. (1965). A Theory of the Allocation of Time. The Economic Journal, 75(299), 493–517. [https://doi.org/10.2307/2228949](https://doi.org/10.2307/2228949
Ironmonger, D. (1996). Counting outputs, capital inputs and caring labor: Estimating gross household product. Feminist Economics, 2(3), 37–64. https://doi.org/10.1080/13545709610001707756
Folbre, N., & Nelson, J. A. (2000). For Love or Money—Or Both? Journal of Economic Perspectives, 14(4), 123–140. https://doi.org/10.1257/jep.14.4.123
Morgan, K. J. (2005). The “Production” of Child Care: How Labor Markets Shape Social Policy and Vice Versa. Social Politics: International Studies in Gender, State & Society, 12(2), 243–263. https://doi.org/10.1093/sp/jxi013
Miller, R., & Sedai, A. K. (2021). Opportunity Costs of Unpaid Caregiving: Evidence from Panel Time Diaries. SSRN Electronic Journal. https://doi.org/10.2139/ssrn.3827577





1. Feedback from the Lab Members

From Chikaraishi Sensei:
Read Takai san’s paper on the preference for driving others on the way to campus and relate it to the effect of personal relationships and emotional attachment on travel behavior.
Consider DDCM (Dynamic Discrete Choice Models) for modeling the decision-making process of caregivers on time allocation and measuring the value of time spent on caregiving activities.
From Reem san:
Explore the use of MDCEV to model the decision-making process on time allocation
From Yaqiu san:
Be more specific on mentioning the outcome and the output of the research in the introduction section of the paper



2. Action Items for Next Meeting

Read papers on MDCEV
Read papers on DDCM
Read papers on driving as a derived demand of other person’s needs
Read papers on the effect of personal relationships and emotional attachment on travel decision
Make a structured list of the questions that I want to include in the improved travel survey and relate them to the findings from the literature

title: Short-term Traffic Prediction Using Statistical Time Series Analysis. A Study Case of National Route 31, Hiroshima, Japan
text: Road traffic possesses a dynamic and anticipatory nature. Previous medium-long interval macro traffic models that require complex data are becoming irrelevant for dealing with the stochastic nature of traffic. Thus, short-term prediction approach come as a solution to model the dynamic and continuity of traffic. This research is structured to formulate short-term traffic flow prediction model alternatives, in which the best-performing model will be used to predict traffic flow at a downstream point on the National Route 31 arterial road, Kure City, Hiroshima, Japan. The models will utilize statistical time series analysis approach, consisted of ARIMA method and Dynamic Regression method. The modeling process resulted in 2 alternative ARIMA models and 10 alternative Dynamic Regression models.
